abstract: Skeletal editing has emerged as a transformative paradigm in synthetic chemistry, offering a surgical approach to the direct remodeling of molecular cores. By enabling the insertion, deletion, or exchange of specific atoms within a molecular framework, this strategy bypasses the resource-intensive requirements of traditional de novo synthesis and streamlines the late-stage diversification of lead compounds. This review provides a comprehensive analysis of recent breakthroughs in the skeletal editing of (hetero)cyclic systems, with a primary focus on single-atom modifications. We categorize the field into three fundamental strategies: ring expansion via atom insertion, ring contraction through atom removal, and atom-swapping transmutations. By contextualizing modern methods—such as carbene-mediated carbon insertion and photo-induced nitrogen deletion—within their historical mechanistic roots, we highlight how these tools are being harnessed to explore chemical space with unprecedented precision. Furthermore, we discuss the burgeoning areas of isotopic exchange and stereochemical editing, addressing current challenges in selectivity and sustainability while outlining the future potential of "molecular surgery" in drug discovery and materials science.
